tip: Use @path/to/import syntax in CLAUDE.md to import additional files. Imported files are expanded and loaded into context at launch alongside the CLAUDE.md that references them.
Create multiple .md files in .claude/rules/ — each can have a paths: YAML frontmatter to scope rules to specific file patterns. Rules without a paths field are loaded unconditionally.
Add .mcp.json to your project root to configure project-scoped MCP servers. This file is designed to be checked into version control, ensuring all team members have access to the same MCP tools.
Use the /memory command to list all loaded CLAUDE.md and rules files, toggle auto memory on or off, and open the auto memory folder. To make Claude remember something, just ask it in conversation.
Type / in Claude Code to open the command menu, then keep typing to filter. The / menu shows both built-in commands and bundled skills like /simplify.
Use .claude/settings.local.json for personal preferences and experimentation. Claude Code will configure git to ignore this file when it is created.
Press Esc twice to open the rewind menu, which shows each prompt from your session. You can restore code, conversation, or both to any previous point, or summarize from a selected message forward.
Use /btw to ask a quick question without adding to the conversation history. It runs independently even while Claude is processing, and answers only from existing context (no tool access).
Start a prompt with ! to run a command directly in Bash mode and add its execution output to the session. Start with @ to trigger file path autocomplete.
Use --bare with claude -p to skip auto-discovery of hooks, skills, plugins, MCP servers, auto memory, and CLAUDE.md. Useful for CI and scripts where you need the same result on every machine.
Run /skills or type $ to mention a skill explicitly in Codex. Codex can also activate a skill automatically when your task matches the skill description.
At each directory level, Codex checks AGENTS.override.md before AGENTS.md and loads only the first match — use it for personal overrides that replace the team file at that level.
Use /mcp to list the MCP servers and tools available in the current Codex session before you ask Codex to use one.
Use /mention followed by a path, such as /mention src/lib/api.ts, then pick the match from the popup. Codex adds that file to the conversation so follow-up turns can refer to it directly.
Define named profiles in config.toml under [profiles.<name>] and switch with codex -p fast or codex -p careful — each can override model, sandbox mode, and approval policy.
Override any config key at runtime with -c key=value using dotted paths (e.g., -c model=o3 or -c shell_environment_policy.inherit=all) — no file edits needed.
Run /review after a coding pass to ask Codex for a working-tree review, then follow up with /diff if you want to inspect the exact edits. /review focuses on behavior changes and missing tests.

Type @path/to/file or @path/to/directory in Gemini CLI to inject that file or directory into your prompt. @ uses git-aware filtering, so ignored folders like node_modules/ and .git/ are skipped by default.
Prefix input with ! to run a shell command directly from Gemini CLI. Type ! on its own to toggle shell mode when you want to fire off several commands in a row.
Use /chat save <tag> before a risky change, then /chat resume <tag> to continue later. Gemini stores those checkpoints per project, so reopen the same repo directory before resuming.
Run /compress when a session gets long. Gemini replaces the full chat context with a summary so future turns use fewer tokens.
Use /memory add <text> to record a decision mid-session, and /memory refresh after editing GEMINI.md files outside the CLI.
Put reusable prompts in ~/.gemini/commands/ for global use or .gemini/commands/ to share them with the repo. A file like .gemini/commands/git/commit.toml becomes the /git:commit command.
Inside a custom Gemini command, !{...} injects shell output and @{...} injects file contents before the prompt is sent. That makes commands like /git:commit or /review much more useful.
Type @Docs in chat to browse indexed documentation. If a library is missing, choose Add new doc, paste the docs URL, and Cursor will index that site and its subpages.
Press Ctrl+T to open a fresh Agent tab for a separate task. Each tab keeps its own history, context, and model, and you can pull context from another thread with @Past Chats.
Use /Generate Cursor Rules in chat when you notice yourself repeating guidance. Cursor can turn that guidance into a reusable project rule instead of making you restate it every session.
Type @Cursor Rules in chat when you want to explicitly apply one of your project rules to the current request.
Put sensitive or irrelevant paths in .cursorignore. Cursor blocks those files from indexing, Tab, Agent, Inline Edit, and @ references, though terminal and MCP tools can still reach them.
Configure project-specific MCP servers in .cursor/mcp.json and global ones in ~/.cursor/mcp.json. In the CLI, run cursor-agent mcp list to see what is currently configured.
Press Cmd/Ctrl+K in the editor to inline-edit selected code. Use Alt+Enter for a quick question first, or Cmd/Ctrl+L to send the selection to chat when the change spans multiple files.

In Cursor terminal, press Cmd/Ctrl+K and describe the command you want in natural language. Cursor generates the terminal command using your recent terminal history and current instructions as context.
Press Cmd + I in the editor or terminal to ask for inline help in natural language. In the editor it can draft code in place, and in the terminal it can suggest commands.

Press Cmd + L to open the agent side panel from the editor. In that chat, use @ to attach files, directories, or MCP servers, and you can also send selected terminal output to the agent with the same shortcut.
Use / in the agent panel to trigger a saved workflow on demand. The codelab shows workspace workflows surfacing as slash commands such as /generate-unit-tests.
Pick Fast for quick localized tasks and Planning when you want the agent to produce a plan you can approve before it edits code.
When the agent edits code, you can Accept all or Reject all directly from the side panel, or open Review changes to inspect diffs and leave comments.
Set Terminal Command Auto Execution to Request Review and use the Allow List for safe commands you want auto-approved. Google's codelab describes this as the positive security model.
Type / in Copilot Chat to browse slash commands for common tasks. In VS Code that includes commands like /fix, /fixTestFailure, /new, and /newNotebook.
Type # in Copilot Chat to attach context such as files, folders, symbols, or tools without rewriting your prompt by hand.
Use #read/terminalLastCommand to attach the last terminal command and its output right after a failed build or test.
Use @terminal for shell-specific questions in chat, and pair it with /explain when you want Copilot to interpret a terminal command or error instead of just suggesting a fix.
Save reusable prompts in .github/prompts/*.prompt.md, then run them from chat with /prompt-name. GitHub's own tutorial shows explain-code.prompt.md becoming /explain-code.

Put repo-wide guidance in .github/copilot-instructions.md and path-specific guidance in .github/instructions/*.instructions.md. Copilot automatically combines them when the current file matches the applyTo glob.
In Copilot CLI, use @relative/path to inject a file, !command to run shell directly, and copilot --continue or /resume to reopen a session with saved context.
Claude Code reads CLAUDE.md, not AGENTS.md. If your repo uses AGENTS.md for other agents, create a CLAUDE.md that imports it with @AGENTS.md so both tools read the same instructions.
Run /init and choose the personal option to create a CLAUDE.local.md for private instructions. It automatically adds the file to .gitignore so it won't be committed.
Add the -p (or --print) flag to any claude command to run it non-interactively. Combine with --output-format json for scripting and CI workflows.
Run /init to auto-generate a starting CLAUDE.md for your project. It scans the codebase and creates a tailored set of initial instructions.
Enable vim-style key bindings for the input editor via /config → Editor mode.
Press Ctrl+T to toggle the task list view, showing all tasks Claude is tracking in the current session.
Use --allowedTools with -p to let Claude use specific tools without prompting for approval, ideal for CI and automation scripts.
Use --continue to pick up the most recent conversation, or --resume with a session ID to return to a specific past session.
Type /hooks in Claude Code to open a read-only browser showing all your configured hooks, their events, and commands.
Use ~/.codex/AGENTS.override.md for a temporary global instruction override without modifying or deleting the base AGENTS.md file.
Start Claude Code with --debug-file <path> to write the debug log to a specific file, or use --debug to write it to ~/.claude/debug/<session-id>.txt.
Run codex --ask-for-approval never "Summarize the current instructions." to verify that Codex is loading your AGENTS.md files correctly — it should quote the items before proposing work.
Use --sandbox (or -s) when launching Gemini CLI to enable sandbox mode for the session, restricting file system and network access.
Type /editor in Gemini CLI to open a dialog for selecting your preferred code editor.
Type /vim in Gemini CLI to toggle vim-style key bindings for the input editor on or off.
Use --approval-mode=yolo instead of the deprecated --yolo flag when launching Gemini CLI for fully autonomous execution.
Use gemini -e none to launch with all extensions disabled, useful for debugging or getting a clean session.
Create custom policy files in ~/.gemini/policies/ (e.g., my-rules.toml) to define fine-grained allow/deny rules for tools and file access.
Press Ctrl+X when Gemini CLI presents a plan for review to open it in your configured external editor for detailed inspection and editing.
Type /memory inbox in Gemini CLI to open the auto-memory inbox and review draft skills extracted from previous sessions before accepting them.
Type /plan [goal] to enter plan mode for a specific objective, or press Shift+Tab from any prompt to cycle between approval modes.
Delete a Gemini session with gemini --delete-session <index|id> from the CLI, or open /resume and press x on the session you want to remove.
Pass --scope workspace to Gemini skill commands to manage workspace-specific skill settings instead of the default user-global scope.
Set GEMINI_SYSTEM_MD=/path/to/my-system.md (absolute or relative to CWD) to load a custom system prompt file in Gemini CLI.
